evidence: JSpecify @Nullable/@NonNull annotations used on constructor parameters, return types, and method parameters throughout (e.g., CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex), QueryExecutor.executeCollocations(@Nullable String lemma), BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch returns @Nullable String)
Sealed interface ConcordanceResult with permits SnippetResult, CollocateResult provides exhaustive subtype tracking at compile time
RelationType, PosGroup, SharingCategory, RelationEdgeType are proper enums used consistently for domain classification
Optional<RelationType> used for optional enum fields in RelationConfig with correct orElseThrow()/orElse() call sites

RelationConfig.name and RelationConfig.description record fields are unannotated (@Nullable absent) despite GrammarConfigSerializer guarding against null: 'if (rel.name() != null) obj.put(...)' — type contract not expressed at the field level

ExplorationHandlers calls .relationType().orElseThrow().name() to extract a raw String relationType which is then passed to buildCoreExploreResponse(String relationType, ...) — the enum is widened to String one method too early; the response-assembly helper accepts String instead of RelationType

VisualizationHandlers uses a raw String comparison 'mode.equals("signed")' with no enum; the only valid non-null mode value implies a one-value domain that was never formalised as an enum or a validated type

parseSeedSet(@NonNull String seedsParam) immediately checks 'if (seedsParam == null || ...)' — the @NonNull annotation and the null guard contradict each other; the annotation signals callers not to pass null but the guard silently accepts it
56 occurrences of Map<String, Object> in the api package for response building — response shape is untyped and verified only at runtime by the JSON serialiser

CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex with a runtime Objects.requireNonNull guard — the Javadoc carves out a 'test subclass' exemption but the annotation widens the visible contract to all callers

strengths: JSpecify @Nullable/@NonNull annotations present on all major public API boundaries, giving callers nullability contracts for key methods
Sealed ConcordanceResult interface with permits clause gives the compiler exhaustive knowledge of all subtype variants
Domain concepts (RelationType, PosGroup, SharingCategory) modelled as enums throughout; no raw-string comparisons for enum-like state except the isolated mode case
Optional<RelationType> used correctly in RelationConfig to model the absent case, with disciplined orElseThrow()/orElse() call sites
Records used for DTOs (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, SnippetResult, HitRecord, SeedCollocateData) eliminating mutable state and providing predictable field types
type_safety.issue_character: The issues are mild and largely structural: enum values are widened to raw Strings one level before serialisation, a handful of record fields lack @Nullable despite defensive null guards at their use sites, and the HTTP response layer uniformly uses Map<String, Object> rather than typed response shapes — a pattern that is repeated across all six handler classes.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase scores well above average on Java type safety fundamentals: JSpecify is in use, enums are the norm, sealed types are exploited correctly, and Optional is used idiomatically. What pulls the score below 80 is a consistent pattern in the API layer where enum values are prematurely stringified (RelationType.name() passed as String through a method boundary), where Map<String, Object> provides no structural guarantee across 56 response-building call sites, and where several RelationConfig record fields carry no nullability annotation despite defensive null checks at serialisation time. These are all real but fixable type-discipline gaps, not deep design flaws — comparable to a codebase at 80=solid but uneven where the unevenness is concentrated in one layer.

issues.summary: RelationType enum widened to raw String one method before serialisation in ExplorationHandlers
evidence: Line 62: String relationType = relationConfig.relationType().orElseThrow().name();
Line 113: String relationType = resolvedConfig.relationType().orElseThrow().name();
Line 217: private Map<String, Object> buildCoreExploreResponse(String relationType, int topCollocates, ...) — accepts String instead of RelationType
issues.suggestion: Change buildCoreExploreResponse to accept RelationType and call .name() only at the point where the value is put into the response map, keeping the enum type through the internal call chain

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers validates mode as a raw String literal rather than a typed enum
evidence: Line 60: if (mode != null && !mode.isEmpty() && !mode.equals("signed")) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown mode: " + mode); }
The string "signed" is the only valid non-null value, implying a one-value domain that is validated only at runtime
issues.suggestion: Introduce a RenderMode enum (SIGNED), parse the JSON string to the enum via a static fromString factory that throws IllegalArgumentException on unknown values, and pass the typed enum to RadialPlot.renderFromItems

issues.summary: RelationConfig record fields name and description lack @Nullable despite defensive null guards at call sites
evidence: RelationConfig record: fields String name, String description, String pattern carry no @Nullable annotation

GrammarConfigSerializer line 47-49: if (rel.name() != null) obj.put(...); if (rel.description() != null) obj.put(...); if (rel.pattern() != null) obj.put(...) — the guards are needed but callers cannot see this from the record signature alone
issues.suggestion: Annotate String name and String description as @Nullable on the RelationConfig record component list; annotate String pattern as @NonNull to match the validation enforced by GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelation which throws if it is blank

issues.summary: parseSeedSet is annotated @NonNull but immediately contains a null guard, contradicting the annotation
evidence: private static Set<String> parseSeedSet(@NonNull String seedsParam) { if (seedsParam == null || seedsParam.isEmpty()) return Set.of(); ... }
@NonNull signals to callers they must not pass null, but the method silently handles null at runtime — the two contract expressions conflict
issues.suggestion: Remove the null branch from parseSeedSet (trust the @NonNull contract and let the caller's requireParam enforce non-null before this point), or if null should be tolerated, replace @NonNull with @Nullable and document the null-returns-empty-set behaviour

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex but all production paths require non-null, widening the visible contract unnecessarily
evidence: CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index) — the Javadoc says null is 'accepted only in test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearchWithContent'
getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearchImpl both have Objects.requireNonNull(index, ...) guards that NPE immediately if index is null in production calls
The @Nullable annotation tells any static analyser that null is a valid argument; in practice it is not for any real caller
issues.suggestion: Replace the @Nullable constructor with a @NonNull parameter for production use and provide a package-private static factory method (e.g. forTesting()) that returns a subclass with overridden methods, keeping null out of the primary constructor path

root_causes: HTTP response assembly using Map<String, Object> rather than typed response objects is a systemic choice that defers all response-shape errors to runtime serialisation; this pattern propagates across all six handler classes

Enum-to-String widening at internal method boundaries suggests an inconsistent convention about when .name() conversion should occur — the rule 'convert only at the JSON serialisation boundary' is not uniformly applied
likely_symptoms: relation_type_enum_to_string_early is symptom-level: the root cause is the lack of a convention about where enum→String conversion belongs
